author: John Chrysostom
book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

title: Homily VII on Acts ii. 37.

source.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Now when they heard these words (E.V. ‘this,’) they were pricked in their heart, and said unto Peter and to the rest of the Apostles, Men and brethren, what shall we do?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you see what a great thing gentleness is?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: More than any vehemence, it pricks our hearts, inflicts a keener wound.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as in the case of bodies which have become callous, the man that strikes upon them does not affect the sense so powerfully, but if he first mollify them and make them tender, then he pierces them effectually; so in this instance also, it is necessary first to mollify.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But that which softens, is not wrath, not vehement accusation, not personal abuse; it is gentleness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The former indeed rather aggravate the callousness, this last alone removes it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If then you are desirous to reprove any delinquent, approach him with all possible mildness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For see here; he gently reminds them of the outrages they have committed, adding no comment; he declares the gift of God, he goes on to speak of the grace which bore testimony to the event, and so draws out his discourse to a still greater length.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So they stood in awe of the gentleness of Peter, in that he, speaking to men who had crucified his Master, and breathed murder against himself and his companions, discoursed to them in the character of an affectionate father and teacher.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not merely were they persuaded; they even condemned themselves, they came to a sense of their past behavior.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he gave no room for their anger to be roused, and darken their judgment, but by means of humility he dispersed, as it were, the mist and darkness of their indignation, and then pointed out to them the daring outrage they had committed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For so it is; when we say of ourselves that we are injured, the opposite party endeavor to prove that they have not done the injury; but when we say, we have not been injured, but have rather done the wrong, the others take the contrary line.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If, therefore, you wish to place your enemy (εἰς ἀγώνα) in the wrong, beware of accusing him; nay (ἀγώνισαι), plead for him, he will be sure to find himself guilty.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There is a natural spirit of opposition in man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such was the conduct of Peter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He did not accuse them harshly; on the contrary, he almost endeavored to plead for them, as far as was possible.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And this was the very reason that he penetrated into their souls.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You will ask, where is the proof that they were pricked?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In their own words; for what say they?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Men and brethren, what shall we do?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whom they had called deceivers, they call “brethren:” not that hereby they put themselves on an equality with them, but rather by way of attracting their brotherly affection and kindness: and besides, because the Apostles had deigned to call them by this title.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And, say they, “What shall we do?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They did not straightway say, Well then, we repent; but they surrendered themselves to the disciples.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Just as a person on the point of shipwreck, upon seeing the pilot, or in sickness the physician, would put all into his hands, and do his bidding in everything; so have these also confessed that they are in extreme peril, and destitute of all hope of salvation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They did not say, How shall we be saved? but, “What shall we do?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here again Peter, though the question is put to all, is the man to answer.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Repent,” says he, “and be baptized every one of you, in the name of Jesus Christ.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He does not yet say, Believe, but, “Be baptized every one of you.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this they received in baptism.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then he speaks of the gain; “For the remission of sins, and ye shall receive the gift of the Holy Ghost.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If you are to receive a gift, if baptism conveys remission, why delay?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He next gives a persuasive turn to his address, adding, “For the promise is unto you” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 39): for he had spoken of a promise above.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And to your children,” he says: the gift is greater, when these are to be heirs of the blessings.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And to all,” he continues, “that are afar off:” if to those that are afar off, much more to you that are near: “even as many as the Lord our God shall call.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe the time he takes for saying, “To those that are afar off.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is when he finds them conciliated and self-accusing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For when the soul pronounces sentence against itself, no longer can it feel envy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And with many other words did he testify, and exhort, saying.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how, throughout, the writer studies brevity, and how free he is from ambition and display.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He testified and exhorted, saying.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is the perfection of teaching, comprising something of fear and something of love.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Save yourselves from this untoward generation.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He says nothing of the future, all is about the present, by which indeed men are chiefly swayed; he shows that the Gospel releases from present evils as well.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then they that gladly received his word were baptized; and the same day there were added unto them about three thousand souls.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Think you not this cheered the Apostles more than the miracle?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they continued steadfastly and with one accord in the Apostles’ doctrine and fellowship.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here are two virtues, perseverance and concord.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “In the Apostles’ doctrine,” he says: for they again taught them; “and fellowship, and in breaking of bread, and in prayer.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: All in common, all with perseverance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And fear came upon every soul” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 43): of those that believed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For they did not despise the Apostles, like common men, nor did they fix their regard on that which was visible merely.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Verily, their thoughts were kindled into a glow.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And as Peter had before spoken much, and declared the promises, and the things to come, well might they be beside themselves with fear.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The wonders also bore witness to the words: “Many wonders and signs were done by the Apostles.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As was the case with Christ; first there were signs, then teaching, then wonders; so was it now.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And all that believed were together, and had all things common.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Consider what an advance was here immediately!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the fellowship was not only in prayers, nor in doctrine alone, but also in (πολιτεία) social relations.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And sold their possessions and goods, and parted them to all men, as every man had need.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See what fear was wrought in them!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they parted them,” he says, showing the (τὸ οἰκονομικὸν) wise management: “As every man had need.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not recklessly, like some philosophers among the Greeks, of whom some gave up their land, others cast into the sea great quantities of money; but this was no contempt of riches, but only folly and madness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For universally the devil has made it his endeavor to disparage the creatures of God, as if it were impossible to make good use of riches.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And continuing daily with one accord in the temple” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 46), they enjoyed the benefit of teaching.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Consider how these Jews did nothing else great or small, than assiduously attend at the temple.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, as having become more earnest, they had increased devotion also to the place.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the Apostles did not for the present pluck them away from this object, for fear of injuring them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And breaking bread from house to house, did take their portion of food with gladness and singleness of heart, praising God, and having favor with all the people.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It seems to me that in mentioning “bread,” he here signifies fasting and hard life; for they “took their portion of food,” not of dainty fare.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “With gladness,” he says.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seest thou that not the dainty fare, but the (τροφἥς οὐ τρυφἥς) food made the enjoyment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For they that fare daintily are under punishment and pain; but not so these.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you see that the words of Peter contain this also, namely, the regulation of life? [“And singleness of heart.”]

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For no gladness can exist where there is no simplicity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How had they “favor with all the people?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: On account of their alms deeds.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For do not look to the fact, that the chief priests for envy and spite rose up against them, but rather consider that “they had favor with the people.”—“And the Lord added to the Church daily (ἐπὶ τὸ αὐτό) [together] such as should be saved.—And all that believed were together.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Once more, the unanimity, the charity, which is the cause of all good things!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation, v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What had been said was not enough.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For those sayings indeed were sufficient to bring them to faith; but these are to show what things the believer behooves to do.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he said not, In the Cross, but, “In the name of Jesus Christ let every one of you be baptized.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he does not put them continually in mind of the Cross, that he may not seem to reproach them, but he says simply, “Repent:” and why?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That we may be punished?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No: “And let each of you be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ, for the remission of sins.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet quite other is the law; of this world’s tribunals: but in the case of the Gospel proclamation (κηρύγματος); when the delinquent has confessed, then is he saved!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how Peter does not instantly hurry over this, but he specifies also the conditions, and adds, “Ye shall receive the gift of the Holy Ghost;” an assertion accredited by the fact, that the Apostles themselves had received that gift. [“For the promise,” etc.] (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The promise,” i.e. the gift of the Holy Ghost.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So far, he speaks of the easy part, and that which has with it a great gift; and then he leads them to practice: for it will be to them a ground of earnestness, to have tasted already of those so great blessings [“and with many other words did he testify,” etc.] (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since, however, the hearer would desire to learn what was the sum and, substance of these further words, he tells us this: [“Saying, save yourselves from this untoward generation.”] [“They then, that gladly received his words,” etc.] (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 41) they approved of what had been said, although fraught with terror, and after their assent given, proceed at once to baptism.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they continued” it is written, “steadfastly in the doctrine” (or, “teaching”) “of the Apostles” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 42): for it was not for one day, no nor for two or three days that they were under teaching as being persons who had gone over to a different course of life. [“And they continued with one accord in the Apostles’ doctrine,” etc.] The expression is not, ὁμοὕ “together,” but ὁμοθυμαδὸν, “with one accord;” (“and daily,” he says [afterwards], “they were continuing with one accord in the temple,”) i.e. with one soul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And here again in his conciseness, he does not relate the teaching given; for as young children, the Apostles nourished them with spiritual food.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And fear came upon every soul” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 43): clearly, of those, as well, who did not believe; namely, upon seeing so great a change all at once effected, and besides in consequence of the miracles. [“And all that believed were together, and had all things in common,” etc.] (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They are all become angels on a sudden; all of them continuing in prayer and hearing, they saw that spiritual things are common, and no one there has more than other, and they speedily came together (ἐπὶ τὸ αὐτὸ), to the same thing in common, even to the imparting to all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And all the believing” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 44), it says, were ἐπὶ τὸ αὐτὸ: and to see that this does not mean that they were together in place, observe what follows [“And had all things common”].

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “All,” it says: not one with the exception of another.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This was an angelic commonwealth, not to call anything of theirs their own.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Forthwith the root of evils was cut out.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By what they did, they showed what they had heard: this was that which he said, “Save yourselves from this untoward generation.”—“And daily continuing with one accord in the temple.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since they are become three thousand, they take them abroad now: and withal, the boldness imparted by the Spirit being great: and daily they went up as to a sacred place, as frequently we find Peter and John doing this: for at present they disturbed none of the Jewish observances.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And this honor too passed over to the place; the eating in the house.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In what house?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the Temple.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe the increase of piety.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They cast away their riches, and rejoiced, and had great gladness, for greater were the riches they received without labor (ἄπονα Cat. al. ἀγαθά).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: None reproached, none envied, none grudged; no pride, no contempt was there.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As children they did indeed account themselves to be under teaching: as new born babes, such was their disposition.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yet why use this faint image?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If you remember how it was when God shook our city with an earthquake, how subdued all men were.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Infra, Hom. xli. §2.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such was the case then with those converts.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No knavery, no villany then: such is the effect of fear, of affliction!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No talk of “mine” and “thine” then.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hence gladness waited at their table; no one seemed to eat of his own, or of another’s;—I grant this may seem a riddle.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Neither did they consider their brethren’s property foreign to themselves; it was the property of a Master; nor again deemed they aught their own, all was the brethren’s.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The poor man knew no shame, the rich no haughtiness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is gladness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The latter deemed himself the obliged and fortunate party; the others felt themselves as honored herein, and closely were they bound together.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For indeed, because when people make doles of money, there are apt to be insults, pride, grudging; therefore says the Apostle, “Not grudgingly, or of necessity.”—(2 Cor. ix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 7.) [“With gladness and simplicity of heart,” etc.] See of how many things he bears witness to them!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Genuine faith, upright conduct, perseverance in hearing, in prayers, in singleness, in cheerfulness. [“Praising God.”] (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Two things there were which might deject them; their abstemious living, and the loss of their property.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yet on both these accounts did they rejoice. [“And having favor with all the people.”]

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For who but must love men of this character, as common fathers?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They conceived no malice toward each other; they committed all to the grace of God. [“With all the people.”]

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Fear there was none; yea, though they had taken their position in the midst of dangers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By singleness, however, he denotes their entire virtue, far surpassing their contempt of riches, their abstinence, and their preseverance in prayer.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For thus also they offered pure praise to God: this is to praise God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But observe also here how they immediately obtain their reward.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Having favor with all the people.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They were engaging, and highly beloved.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For who would not prize and admire their simplicity of character; who would not be linked to one in whom was nothing underhand?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To whom too does salvation belong, but to these?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To whom those great marvels?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Was it not to shepherds that the Gospel was first preached? and to Joseph, being a man of simple mind, insomuch that he did not let a suspicion of adultery frighten him into doing wrong?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Did not God elect rustics, those artless men?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is written, “Blessed is every simple soul.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And again, “He that walketh simply, walketh surely.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “True,” you will say, “but prudence also is needed.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, what is simplicity, I pray you, but prudence?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For when you suspect no evil, neither can you fabricate any: when you have no annoyances, neither can you remember injuries.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Has any one insulted you?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You were not pained.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Has any one reviled you?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You were nothing hurt.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Has he envied you?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Still you had no hurt.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Simplicity is a high road to true philosophy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: None so beautiful in soul as the simple.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as in regard of personal appearance, he that is sullen, and downcast, and reserved (σύννους), even if he be good-looking, loses much of his beauty; while he that relaxes his countenance, and gently smiles, enhances his good looks; so in respect of the soul, he that is reserved, if he have ten thousand good points, disfigures them; but the frank and simple, just the reverse.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: A man of this last description may be safely made a friend, and when at variance easily reconciled.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No need of guards and outposts, no need of chains and fetters with such an one; but great is his own freedom, and that of those who associate with him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But what, you will say, will such a man do if he fall among wicked people?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: God, Who has commanded us to be simple-minded, will stretch out His hand.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What was more guileless than David?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What more wicked than Saul?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yet who triumphed?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, in Joseph’s case; did not he in simplicity approach his master’s wife, she him with wicked art?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yet what, I pray, was he the worse?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Furthermore, what more simple than was Abel? what more malicious than Cain?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Joseph again, had he not dealt artlessly with his brethren?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Was not this the cause of his eminence, that he spoke out unsuspiciously, while they received his words in malice?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He declared once and again his dreams unreservedly; and then again he set off to them carrying provisions; he used no caution; he committed all to God: nay, the more they held him in the light of an enemy, the more did he treat them as brothers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: God had power not to have suffered him to fall into their hands; but that the wonder might be made manifest, how, though they do their worst, he shall be higher than they: though the blow do come upon him, it comes from another, not from himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: On the contrary, the wicked man strikes himself first, and none other than himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For alone,” it is said, “shall he bear his troubles.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Ever in him the soul is full of dejection, his thoughts being ever entangled: whether he must hear aught or say aught, he does all with complaints, with accusation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Far, very far from such do friendship and harmony make their abode: but fightings are there, and enmities, and all unpleasantness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They that are such suspect even themselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To these not even sleep is sweet, nor anything else.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And have they a wife also, lo, they are enemies and at war with all: what endless jealousies, what unceasing fear!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Aye, the wicked, πονηρὸς has his name from πονεἵν, “to have trouble.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And, indeed, thus the Scripture is ever calling “wickedness” by the name of labor; as, for instance, “Under his tongue is toil and labor;” and again, “In the midst of them is toil and labor.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now if any one should wonder, whence those who had at first been of this last class, now are so different, let him learn that affliction was the cause, affliction, that school-mistress of heavenly wisdom, that mother of piety.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When riches were done away with, wickedness also disappeared.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: True, say you, for this is the very thing I am asking about; but whence comes all the wickedness there is now?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How is it that it came into the minds of those three thousand and five thousand straightway, to choose virtue, and that they simultaneously became Christian philosophers, whereas now hardly one is to be found? how was it that they then were in such harmony?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What was it, that made them resolute and active?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What was it that so suddenly inflamed them?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The reason is, that they drew near with much piety; that honors were not so sought after as they are now; that they transferred their thoughts to things future, and looked for nothing of things present.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is the sign of an ardent mind, to encounter perils; this was their idea of Christianity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We take a different view, we seek our comfort here.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The result is, that we shall not even obtain this, when the time is come.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “What are we to do?” asked those men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We, just the contrary—“What shall we do?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What behooved to be done, they did.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We, quite the reverse.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Those men condemned themselves, despaired of saving themselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is what made them such as they were.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They knew what a gift they had received.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But how can you become like them, when you do everything in an opposite spirit?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They heard, and were forthwith baptized.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They did not speak those cold words which we do now, nor did they contrive delays (p.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 47, note 3); and yet they had heard all the requirements: but that word, “Save yourselves from this generation,” made them to be not sluggish; rather they welcomed the exhortation; and that they did welcome it, they proved by their deeds, they showed what manner of men they were.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They entered at once the lists, and took off the coat; whereas we do enter, but we intend to fight with our coat on.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is the cause that our antagonist has so little trouble, for we get entangled in our own movements, and are continually thrown down.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We do precisely the same thing as he who, having to cope with a man frantic, breathing fire; and seeing him, a professed wrestler, covered with dust, tawny, stripped, clotted with dirt from the sand and sun, and running down with sweat and oil and dirt; himself, smelling of perfumes, should put on his silken garments, and his gold shoes, and his robe hanging down to his heels, and his golden trinkets on the head, and so descend into the arena, and grapple with him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such a one will not only be impeded, but being taken up with the sole idea of not staining or rending his fine clothes, will tumble at the very first onset, and withal will suffer that which he chiefly dreaded, the damage of those his fond delights.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The time for the contest is come, and say, are you putting on your silks?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is the time of exercise, the hour of the race, and are you adorning yourself as for a procession?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Look not to outward things, but to the inward.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For by the thoughts about these things the soul is hampered on all sides, as if by strong cords, so that she cannot let you raise a hand, or contend against the adversary; and makes you soft and effeminate.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: One may think himself, even when released from all these ties, well off, to be enabled to conquer that impure power.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And on this account Christ too did not allow the parting with riches alone to suffice, but what saith He?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Sell whatsoever thou hast, and give to the poor, and come and follow Me.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now if, even when we cast away our riches, we are not yet in a safe position, but stand still in need of some further art and close practice; much more, if we retain them, shall we fail to achieve great things, and, instead thereof, become a laughing-stock to the spectators, and to the evil one himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For even though there were no devil, though there were none to wrestle with us, yet ten thousand roads on all sides lead the lover of money to hell.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Where now are they who ask why the devil was made (διατί ὁ δ. γέγονεν;)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Behold here the devil has no hand in the work, we do it all ourselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of a truth they of the hills might have a right to speak thus, who after they had given proof of their temperance, their contempt of wealth and disregard of all such things, have infinitely preferred to abandon father, and houses, and lands, and wife, and children.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yet, they are the last to speak so: but the men who at no time ought to say it, these do say it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Those are indeed wrestlings with the devil; these he does not think worth entering into.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You will say, But it is the devil who instils this same covetousness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well, flee from it, do not harbor it, O man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Suppose now, you see one flinging out filth from some upper story, and at the same time a person seeing it thrown out, yet standing there and receiving it all on his head: you not only do not pity him, but you are angry, and tell him it serves him right; and, “Do not be a fool,” everyone cries out to him, and lays the blame not so much on the other for shooting out the filth, as on him for letting it come on him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But now, you know that covetousness is of the devil; you know that it is the cause of ten thousand evils; you see him flinging out, like filth, his noisome imaginations; and do you not see that you are receiving on your bare head his nastiness, when it needed but to turn aside a little to escape it altogether?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Just as our man by shifting his position would have escaped; so, do you refuse to admit such imaginations, ward off the lust.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And how am I to do this? you will ask.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Were you a Gentile, and had eyes for things present alone, the matter perhaps might be one of considerable difficulty, and yet even the Gentiles have achieved as much; but you—a man in expectation of heaven and heavenly bliss—and you to ask, “How am I to repel bad thoughts?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Were I saying the contrary, then you might doubt: did I say, covet riches, “How shall I covet riches,” you might answer, “seeing such things as I do?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Tell me, if gold and precious stones were set before you, and I were to say, Desire lead, would there not be reason for hesitation?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For you would say, How can I?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if I said, Do not desire it; this had been plainer to understand.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I do not marvel at those who despise, but at those who despise not riches.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is the character of a soul exceeding full of stupidity, no better than flies and gnats, a soul crawling upon the earth, wallowing in filth, destitute of all high ideas.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What is it you say?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Are you destined to inherit eternal life; and do you say, how shall I despise the present life for the future?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What, can the things be put in competition?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You are to receive a royal vest; and say you, How shall I despise these rags?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You are going to be led into the king’s palace; and do you say, How shall I despise this present hovel?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of a truth, we ourselves are to blame in every point, we who do not choose to let ourselves be stirred up ever so little.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the willing have succeeded, and that with great zeal and facility.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Would that you might be persuaded by our exhortation, and succeed too, and become imitators of those who have been successful, through the grace and mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ, with Whom to the Father and the Holy Ghost together be glory, and power, and honor, now and ever, and world without end.
